title: rootless podman doesn't work on WSL2 after system restart
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

If you run a container on WSL2 using `podman run -d -p 8080:80 nginx` and restart (or sign out from) your windows PC, you get an error message after every podman command, until you delete or restart a couple of times this container.

```
ERRO[0000] error joining network namespace for container 88a8d5c7115598aeaa31fcd1cee8c084fee3ab2577b4f61dc317053d7da032f9: error retrieving network namespace at /tmp/podman-run-1000/netns/cni-f73b0b0b-155d-3c43-30b2-278280c003f1: unknown FS magic on "/tmp/podman-run-1000/netns/cni-f73b0b0b-155d-3c43-30b2-278280c003f1": ef53
Error: error joining network namespace of container 88a8d5c7115598aeaa31fcd1cee8c084fee3ab2577b4f61dc317053d7da032f9: error retrieving network namespace at /tmp/podman-run-1000/netns/cni-f73b0b0b-155d-3c43-30b2-278280c003f1: unknown FS magic on "/tmp/podman-run-1000/netns/cni-f73b0b0b-155d-3c43-30b2-278280c003f1": ef53
```

The same happens if you run a pod using the command `podman play kube filename.yaml`. In this case you need to remove the pod or restart it a couple of times to get rid of the error.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Install WSL2 and podman inside WSL2 on Windows PC

2. Run `podman run -d -p 8080:80 nginx`

3. Sign out from Windows PC (or restart it)

4. Run `podman ps`

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

There is a workaround:
_Originally posted by @alexcern in https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/6800#issuecomment-943288170_

> My understanding is there is no systemd/init.d at all; it seems we can run things at startup via the Windows scheduler ([example](https://techbrij.com/wsl-2-ubuntu-services-windows-10-startup)) but that's not very convenient.
> 
> In my case I made do with assuming that `/proc/1` being more recent than the temp folder indicated a reboot took place. I added this to my `.bashrc`:
> 
> ```shell
> function refresh_rootless_podman_after_reboot {
>   local libpod_tmp="/tmp/run-$(id -u)/libpod/tmp"
>   if [ /proc/1 -nt "${libpod_tmp}" ]; then
>     rm -rf "${libpod_tmp}" 
>   fi
> }
> refresh_rootless_podman_after_reboot
> ```
> 
> But a simpler solution could be to include the boot ID in the temp path so we always get a fresh folder after reboot, e.g `export XDG_RUNTIME_DIR="/tmp/run-$(id -u)/$(cat /proc/sys/kernel/random/boot_id)"`

In our project we use `podman play kube filename.yaml` to start the pod, so this workaround works only after adding `podman pod restart -all` command 3 times after the function (the first 2 restarts return an error)

```bash
function refresh_rootless_podman_after_reboot {
  local libpod_tmp="/tmp/run-$(id -u)/libpod/tmp"
  if [ /proc/1 -nt "${libpod_tmp}" ]; then
    rm -rf "${libpod_tmp}"
  fi
}
refresh_rootless_podman_after_reboot

podman pod restart --all
podman pod restart --all
podman pod restart --all
```

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      3.3.1
API Version:  3.3.1
Go Version:   go1.16.6
Built:        Thu Jan  1 01:00:00 1970
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**
Ubuntu 20.04.3 LTS on WSL2 on Windows 11

Tried also with Ubuntu 18.04 on WSL, with Debian on WSL and with Podman 3.0.1

comments.body:
I recommend to mount a tmpfs on /tmp. Having the tmp files available after a reboot results in all sort of bugs. e.g. `podman ps` will show wrong information because it thinks your containers are still running.
@mheon Any other ideas?

At some point during system restart, `/tmp/podman-run-1000/alive` has to be removed, so we detect the restart. Recommended way of doing that is a tmpfs, as @Luap99 said, but a script doing it (so long as that script ran only once, and only ran immediately after a restart) would also work.

For now, you can manually remove the file, which will cause a restart to be detected and should make Podman fully usable again.
Don't see how Podman can fix this issue. Closing.

@Luap99 What do you mean with **mount a tmpfs on /tmp** ?
In my case I tired with `podman run -d --name nginx -p 8080:80 nginx` then `wsl --shutdown`
After that `podman ps -a` show the same message but the container is not running anyway.
I can do a `podman stop nginx`.  Then `podman ps -a ` works back normally but the container cannot be started again.
The only solution is to remove it an recreate.

The /tmp files should be on a temporary filesystem. So you either remove the /tmp/... files after each boot before you run any podman commands or the easier solution is to mount a temporary filesystem which only exists in ram so it is automatically deleted for each boot. You can run this command to do it: `mount -t tmpfs none /tmp`, on normal linux distros you can set this in `/etc/fstab` so that this mountpoint is always created on boot, I have no idea if this works on wsl.

So I removed the `/tmp` folder.
I edited the fstab to mount tmpfs on /tmp.
I seems working.  I created a file in /tmp.  After reboot /tmp is empty.
I tired again `podman run -d --name nginx -p 8080:80 nginx`
Reboot.
`podman ps -a` ... is working without any message !
Of course the container is not started but I can do it with `podman start nginx`.
It is full working.
Many thanks.
Had the same issue. Implemented the tmpfs mount @Luap99 posted

Here is how I set fstab to mount  tmpfs, it works.
I post it here for others who don't konw how to configure fstab.
```bash
echo "none  /tmp  tmpfs  defaults  0 0" | sudo tee -a /etc/fstab
```

create /tmp folder if not exist
```
sudo mkdir /tmp
sudo chmod 1777 /tmp
```

I am running podman in WSL2 and I got this error after I've restarted the host. The solution for me was to run in powershell on the host the command: `podman machine start`

Maybe it will help someone that runs into this for the same reason as me.

> Don't see how Podman can fix this issue. Closing.

May be not writing persistent data to a temporary folder ? Why such info are not written into ~/.local/share ?
